title: Grease on Denim
stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (lipid-based). Grease stains are hydrophobic triglycerides and fatty acids that resist water-based cleaning. They penetrate fiber interstices through capillary action and adhere through van der Waals forces. Polymerization occurs with heat (oxidative cross-linking), making stains permanent if heat-set. On denim (cellulose fiber): tolerates most chemicals well. Treatment: dry-side solvent (POG) to dissolve lipid phase, followed by surfactant emulsification to remove residual oil.
whyThisWorks: Cooking grease is a thick oil that coats surfaces and resists water — water alone won't break it down. Denim is thick and tightly woven so the grease sits near the surface longer before soaking through. NSD or a degreaser breaks down the oil so it becomes water-soluble and can be rinsed away.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG directly to the oil stain. POG is a volatile dry solvent with strong affinity for lipids — it dissolves oil droplets on contact. Tamp gently and blot with absorbent cloth. Dwell 5–10 minutes.

2. If POG plateaus, apply amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. Amyl acetate is especially effective on older or polymerized oil stains. Blot with clean absorbent cloth between applications. Never use on acetate fabrics. Dwell 3–5 minutes.
3. Apply NSD to emulsify any remaining oil micro-droplets and remove solvent residue. Rinse thoroughly with cool water. Dwell 5 minutes.
4. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to restore fiber to its natural state. Blot dry and allow to air dry. Dwell 1–2 minutes.
professionalProtocol.temperature: warm for rinsing — warm water helps emulsify residual oil; cold during solvent phase
warnings: Rubbing instead of blotting — spreads the stain
Not testing in an inconspicuous area first
Using too aggressive a chemical without checking surface compatibility

steps: 1. Sprinkle baking soda or cornstarch on the stain. Wait 5 minutes.
2. Brush off the powder with a clean white cloth.
3. Put a drop of Dawn NSD on the stain. Blot it in.
4. Let it sit for 2–3 minutes. Rinse with cold water.
5. Repeat if needed. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Try a dry cleaning solvent (Carbona Stain Devil #5 or Goo Gone) — apply and let it soak for 30–60 minutes. If the stain survives two rounds, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Never add water to an oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, and you'll spread it.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone.

neverDo: Apply heat before the oil is removed — polymerizes the grease permanently into the fiber
Add water to a fresh oil stain first — spreads it before any treatment can work
fiberSensitivities: On denim: test all agents in an inconspicuous area first. Monitor for color change, surface dulling, or texture damage during treatment.

escalation.when: Grease stain remains after POG and amyl acetate treatment — likely old or polymerized
escalation.whatToTell: Cooking grease stain on denim, oil-based. Treated at home with NSD. Please use professional dry-side solvent protocol.

contextQuestions: Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting and drying?
Has the denim been through the dryer with the stain present?
What type of grease — cooking oil, butter, animal fat, mechanic grease?
Is the denim dark (indigo) or light washed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dryer exposure polymerizes the oil and makes removal much harder. Mechanic or petroleum grease is heavier and more tenacious than cooking oil. Dark denim: test all solvents on a hidden seam first.
